llms.txt

Theme Architecture

Outpost themes are directories of Liquid-style .html template files. There is no PHP in themes — you write plain HTML with template tags, and Outpost compiles it.

How themes work

Outpost's theme system is built around a simple idea: your templates should look like HTML, not PHP. You write .html files with Liquid-style tags ({{ field }}, {% for %}, {% if %}), and Outpost's template engine compiles them into cached PHP at runtime.

This separation has a practical benefit for site builders: you can hand a theme to a designer who has never touched PHP, and they can read and edit the templates without confusion. The compiled PHP lives in a cache directory and is never meant to be touched directly.

The compile step happens automatically on first request — and again any time you modify a template file. Outpost compares the file modification time of the .html source against the cached .php output, and recompiles if the source is newer. You never need to manually trigger a build.

Template engine pipeline

When a request comes in for a page, the following happens:

  1. The router determines which .html template file corresponds to the URL.
  2. The template engine checks whether a compiled cache exists at outpost/cache/templates/{hash}.php.
  3. If the cache is stale or missing, the .html source is compiled into PHP and written to the cache directory.
  4. The compiled PHP is required, executing the template with the current page context.

The hash used for the cache filename is an MD5 of the template file's real path, ensuring each template gets a stable, unique cache entry regardless of how the theme is organized.

Note — Partials are compiled and cached independently. When a template includes a partial, the partial gets its own cache entry. This means changing a partial invalidates only that partial's cache, not every template that uses it.

Theme directory structure

A theme lives in its own directory under outpost/themes/. The directory name becomes the theme's slug and is used internally to reference the theme. Here is a complete example structure:

outpost/themes/my-theme/
  theme.json            ← required — display name, version, author, description
  index.html            ← / route (homepage)
  about.html            ← /about route
  blog.html             ← /blog route
  post.html             ← collection single (e.g. /post/{slug})
  contact.html          ← /contact route
  404.html              ← optional custom 404 page
  partials/
    head.html           ← standard: DOCTYPE through <body> opening tag
    nav.html            ← standard: site navigation
    footer.html         ← standard: </body></html> and scripts
    sidebar.html        ← custom partial example
  assets/
    style.css
    main.js
    logo.svg

URL routing overview

Template files map directly to URL paths. The router converts a URL path into a filename by taking the first path segment and appending .html:

URLTemplate fileNotes
/index.htmlHomepage
/aboutabout.htmlStatic page
/blogblog.htmlCollection list page
/post/hello-worldpost.htmlCollection single; ?slug=hello-world set automatically
/contactcontact.htmlStatic page
no match404.htmlFalls back to built-in 404 if file missing

Collection URL patterns (configured in Admin → Collections → URL Pattern) tell the router which two-segment URLs belong to a collection. For example, setting the pattern to /post/{slug} means any request matching /post/* is routed to post.html with the slug passed as a query parameter.

See Routing for the full algorithm including draft blocking, visibility, and the difference between the dev server and production.

Field auto-registration

One of Outpost's most useful features is that you never have to manually define fields in the admin before using them in a template. When a template is compiled for the first time, Outpost scans all the template tags and registers any new fields it finds in the database.

This means the workflow is: write your template, activate the theme, load the page once — and the fields appear in the admin ready to be filled in. You do not need to visit a "field settings" screen before the template works.

The same auto-registration happens for global fields ({{ @global_name }}), meta tags, and collection fields. The field type is inferred from the filter used — {{ hero_image | image }} registers as an image picker, {{ bio | textarea }} registers as a textarea, and so on.

Tip — If you remove a field tag from a template, the field data in the database is not deleted — it simply stops appearing in the admin editor for that page. This is intentional: removing a field from a template is non-destructive. If you add the tag back later, the previously entered content will still be there.

Active theme

Only one theme is active at a time. The active theme is stored in the settings table under the key active_theme. You can change it through Admin → Themes → Activate, or by directly updating that database value.

When you activate a theme, Outpost immediately scans all its template files and scaffolds any fields that do not yet exist in the database. This means activating a brand-new theme on an existing site is safe — it creates the new field stubs without touching any existing content.

Multiple themes

You can have as many themes installed as you like. Only the active theme is rendered for visitors. The others sit in outpost/themes/ untouched. This makes it easy to:

  • Stage a redesign as a new theme and switch over when ready
  • Keep a backup copy of the previous theme
  • Offer multiple theme options for a multi-site setup

Theme duplication

The admin provides a Duplicate button on each theme card. Duplicating a theme creates a complete copy — all template files, partials, and assets — under a new directory slug. The duplicate gets a new name based on the original (e.g., my-theme-copy).

Duplication is the recommended way to start a customization. Duplicate the base theme, activate the copy, and edit freely. The original stays intact as a reference or rollback point.

Warning — Deleting a theme removes its directory from disk. This cannot be undone. Make sure you are not deleting the active theme — Outpost will prevent this, but always double-check before deleting.

Legacy PHP themes

If a theme directory contains an index.php file at its root, Outpost treats it as a legacy PHP theme and requires that file directly, bypassing the Liquid compiler entirely. This exists for backward compatibility only. All new themes should use Liquid .html templates.

There is no migration tool to convert a PHP theme to Liquid — but in practice the conversion is straightforward: replace PHP echo calls with {{ }} tags and PHP control structures with {% %} blocks.
